does: This paper constructs a comprehensive framework for European AI sovereignty by mapping five sovereignty pillars (economic competitiveness, resilience, security and defence, European values, and foreign relations) against a detailed decomposition of the frontier AI stack comprising five layers, 26 components, and 29 sub-components. The authors document the EU's structural dependence on US and Chinese frontier models, noting the US holds approximately sixteen times greater AI supercomputing capacity than the EU and only 15% of global hyperscale data centre capacity resides within EU borders. The framework systematically identifies critical gaps, redundancies, and inter-pillar trade-offs that existing fragmented EU policy initiatives fail to address, enabling explicit analysis of where strategic autonomy remains absent across the frontier AI value chain.
connects: This work responds to growing concerns about geopolitical dependence on foreign AI capabilities and builds on earlier policy analysis examining EU technological sovereignty. It extends prior decomposition methodologies used in critical infrastructure assessment and strategic autonomy frameworks to the specific domain of frontier AI systems. The paper engages with ongoing European Commission policy acceleration efforts while critiquing their fragmentation, connecting to broader debates about digital sovereignty and strategic autonomy that have emphasized supply chain resilience, computational capacity, and data control. It draws implicitly on defense and economics literatures examining strategic dependence and critical technology clusters.
enables: This framework enables targeted policy interventions by making implicit trade-offs and dependencies explicit across the AI value chain, supporting more coherent EU strategic planning. It provides a foundation for identifying priority investment areas, whether in chip manufacturing, alternative model development, data infrastructure, or talent development. The componentized stack view could guide resource allocation decisions and international partnerships while the sovereignty pillar mapping allows policymakers to evaluate whether initiatives genuinely address multiple objectives or create brittle single-pillar solutions. The work potentially enables future comparative analysis of different nations' sovereignty approaches and could support emerging discussions about defensive AI capabilities, data governance, and European technological leadership in specific frontier domains.
